title: A Modulation Layer to Increase Neural Network Robustness Against Data Quality Issues

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - A MODULATION LAYER TO INCREASE NEURAL NET WORK ROBUSTNESS AGAINST DATA QUALITY ISSUES

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Data quality is a common problem in machine learning, especially in high-stakes settings such as healthcare. Missing data affects accuracy, calibration, and feature attribution in complex patterns. Developers often train models on carefully curated datasets to minimize missing data bias; however, this reduces the usability of such models in production environments, such as real-time healthcare records. Making machine learning models robust to missing data is therefore crucial for practical applications. While some classifiers naturally handle missing data, others, such as deep neural networks, are not designed for unknown values. We propose a novel neural network modification to mitigate the impacts of missing data. The approach is inspired by neuromodulation that is performed by biological neural networks. Our proposal replaces the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (reliability score) at each input, mimicking the ability of cortex to up- and down-weight inputs based on the presence of other data. The modulation function is jointly learned with the main task using a multilayer perceptron. We tested our modulating fully connected layer on multiple classification, regression, and imputation problems, and it either improved performance or generated comparable performance to conventional neural network architectures concatenating reliability to the inputs. Models with modulating layers were more robust against degradation of data quality by introducing additional missingness at evaluation time. These results suggest that explicitly accounting for reduced information quality with a modulating fully connected layer can enable the deployment of artificial intelligence systems in real-time settings.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Despite the enormous academic and industrial interest in artificial intelligence, there is a large gap between model performance in laboratory settings and real-world deployments. Reports estimate that over 75% of data science and artificial intelligence projects do not make it into production (VentureBeat, 2019; Sagar, 2021; Chen and Asch, 2017). One difficult transition from the laboratory is handling noisy and missing data. Errors in predictor data and labels (Northcutt et al., 2021) at the training stage are well understood to produce poor pattern recognition with any strategy; garbage-in garbage-out. In the statistical learning literature, the effects of inaccurate and missing data on simple classifiers such as logistic regression is particularly well understood (Ameisen, 2020). As a result, datasets intended to train high-accuracy models are often carefully curated and reviewed for validity (Ameisen, 2020; Xiao et al., 2018). However, when faced with noisy data from a new source, these models may fail (L’Heureux et al., 2017). One special case is convolutional neural networks for machine vision; augmenting the dataset with partially obscured inputs has been shown to increase the network’s ability to match low-level patterns and increases accuracy (Zhong et al., 2020). No similar results with masking have been shown in tabular data, to our knowledge.

These challenges are even more pronounced in applications that require high reliability and feature pervasive missing data at inference time, such as healthcare (Chen and Asch, 2017; Xiao et al., 2018). Electronic health records (EHR) can contain a high percentage of missing data both at random (keyboard entry errors, temporarily absent data due to incomplete charting) and informative or missing-not-at-random (MNAR) data (selective use of lab tests or invasive monitors based on

observed or unobserved patient characteristics). Medical measurements also have non-uniform noise; for instance, invasive blood pressure measurement is more accurate than non-invasive blood pressure (Kallioinen et al., 2017). Another example is the medical equipment by different manufacturers that have various margins of error which affects the accuracy and hence the reliability of the measurement (Patel et al., 2007).

Mammalian brains have a distinct strategy to integrate multi-modal data to generate a model of the surrounding environment. They modify the impact of each input based on the presence and reliability of other signals. This effect can be observed dynamically in response to temporary changes in available inputs (Shine et al., 2019), as well as long-term as a compensation mechanism for permanent changes such as neural injuries (Hylin et al., 2017). For example, a human brain gives less weight to visual input in a dark environment and relies on prior knowledge and other sensory cues more. Unlike simply down-weighting low-accuracy data, replacement data with related information is up-weighted. This is usually modelled as a Bayesian inference process (Cao et al., 2019; Ernst and B¨ulthoff, 2004; Alais and Burr, 2004; Heeger, 2017). This modulation of different input is also observed in other organisms where the neural behavior of a neuron or a group of neurons can be altered using neuromodulators (Harris-Warrick and Marder, 1991). We used the inspiration from this process to design a fully-connected neural network layer with variable weights. Those weights could be modulated based on a variety of inputs, but we focus on input reliability as a modulating signal. This allowed us to train the neural network using datasets that are loosely preprocessed with a high incidence of missing data while achieving high performance. At inference time, the network is capable of producing accurate outputs despite signal degradation. A restricted structure of modulating inputs and effects on the modulated layer reduces the likelihood of severe over-fitting and complexity of the estimation problem.

## 2 RELATED WORK

The most obvious use case we propose for this structure is handling missing data. There is a vast literature on imputation, which also attempts to use alternative inputs to replace missing data. Classical simple methods of imputation include constant values (e.g. mean imputation), hot deck, k-nearest neighbor, and others (Buck, 1960). Single or multiple imputation using chained equations (Gibbs sampling of missing data) is popular due to its relative accuracy and ability to account for imputation uncertainty (Azur et al., 2011). More advanced yet classic methods have seen relative success such as Bayesian ridge regression (MacKay, 1992) and random forest imputation (Stekhoven and B¨uhlmann, 2012). Deep learning-based imputation has been used recently using generative networks (Beaulieu-Jones and Moore, 2017; McCoy et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Lall and Robinson, 2021; McCoy et al., 2018; Mattei and Frellsen, 2019; Yoon et al., 2018; Ivanov et al., 2019) and graph networks (You et al., 2020). Our modulation approach can be incorporated into autoencoder architectures to improve their performance and stability in data imputation, but it also provides the flexibility of skipping the imputation step altogether when the task performed does not require imputation (i.e. classification) thus skipping a preprocessing step and saving processing time.

Incorporating uncertainty measurements into deep neural networks has also been approached with Bayesian deep learning methods, (Wang and Yeung, 2016; Wilson, 2020) which has a complex, assumption laden structure using probabilistic graphical models. One simpler variation of Bayesian deep learning is the Gaussian process deep neural network which assigns an uncertainty level at the output based on the missing data so that inputs with greater missingness lead to higher uncertainty (Bradshaw et al., 2017). Our method makes use of meaningful missingness patterns as opposed to treating it as a problem that leads to lower confidence in outputs. Our approach is superficially similar to attention mechanisms: the lower quality inputs receive less importance, but attention networks employ a complex feedback mechanism to assign the attention distribution using the input sequence and the query and is thus difficult to scale for long time-varying inputs (Kim et al., 2017).

## 3 METHODS

## 3.1 ARCHITECTURE

A fully connected layer has a transfer function of

_hout_ = _f_ ( **W** _· hin_ + _b_ ) _,_ (1)

where _hin_ is the input to the layer, **W** is the weight matrix , **b** the bias and _f_ the non-linearity function. **W** is optimized during training and fixed at inference. We propose a modulated fully connected layer (MFCL) where weights are made variable by replacing **W** by **Wmod** (Figure 1) where

**Wmod** = _g_ ( _m_ ) _,_ (2)

where _m_ is the modulating signal input and _g_ is the function that is defined by a multilayer perceptron. Another variant of the MFCL (MFCL+) adds a skip connection to the transfer step and modifies the weights ( **W0** ) of a starting network,

**Wmod** + = _g_ ( _m_ ) + **W0** _._ (3)

The latter architecture aims at being more adaptive to datasets with less or no missing values and could avoid potential instabilities by having the entire layer weights variable.

## 3.2 EXPERIMENTS

We assessed the performance of the MFCL and MFCL+ layers in classification, regression, and imputation tasks. These experiments used modulating signals of missing value flags and input reliability values of noisy data. We can think of missing values as a special case included in reliability where _missing_ implies completely unreliable measurement. For the sake of clarity, we test the cases of missing values and noisy values separately rather than combining them. For baseline comparison, we employed models with matching architectures while swapping the first fullyconnected layer with a MFCL/MFCL+. Base architectures were guided by previous best performing models in the literature. Modulation network architectures were optimized using a grid search. A complete description of the architectures is elaborated in the appendix.

## 3.3 DATASETS

The motivating dataset for our experiments derives from operating room data from Barnes Jewish Hospital’s Anesthesiology Control Tower project (ACTFAST) spanning 2012–2018. The [Name redacted for anonymity] IRB approved this study and granted a waiver of informed consent. The dataset contains preoperative measurements of medical conditions, demographics, vital signs, and lab values of patients as well as postoperative outcomes that were used as labels for supervised learning including 30-day mortality, acute kidney injury, and heart attack. The ACTFAST dataset was used in previous studies for prediction of 30day mortality (Fritz et al., 2019), acute kidney injury, and

other complications (Cui et al., 2019; Abraham et al., 2021; Fritz et al., 2018). For predictors, we utilized a subset of the input features of preoperative vital signs and lab values (15 variables). Table 1 (appendix) shows a list of variables used and the missing percentages. Table 2 (appendix) shows the distribution of outcome values, which have a large imbalance between positive and negative samples. We also used the Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset for classification of tumors from features extracted from a fine needle aspirate of breast mass image (Mangasarian et al., 1995). We used the Boston housing prices dataset (Harrison Jr and Rubinfeld, 1978) as a regression example. Each of the above datasets was also used for imputation tasks.

## 3.4 CLASSIFICATION TASK

We ran five experiments for classification using the ACTFAST and Breast Cancer datasets. Four experiments utilized the missing flags as a modulating signal; in the last experiment, we utilized input reliability as a modulating signal. Reliability was quantified by the standard deviation of the noise that was artificially added to the signal. We tested the MFCL and MFCL+ in the place of fully-connected (FC) layers at the input level.

## 3.4.1 BASELINES

The baseline classifiers for the ACTFAST and Breast Cancer datasets were four MLPs with matching hidden layer structures that were fed imputed values using different algorithms, namely: chained regression with Bayesian ridge regularization (Scikit Learn Iterative Imputer), missForest algorithm (Stekhoven and B¨uhlmann, 2012), GAIN (Yoon et al., 2018), and VAEAC (Ivanov et al., 2019). We also tested one graphical network model GRAPE (You et al., 2020). The last model applied constant value imputation (mean value) in addition to concatenating an indicator variable for missing values at the input layer (FC+Mod).

**ACTFAST** We built three classifiers to predict 30-day Mortality, Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), and Heart Attack from the preoperative input features (Table 1). We used the datasets with the inherent missing data for training and then tested the trained models with additional missingness artificially introduced in both random and non-random fashions. Non-random missingness was introduced by removing the largest values and by all datapoints of features (discussed below).

**Breast Cancer** For the classifier with missing flags as modulating signal, we introduced nonrandom missingness into the training dataset by removing the highest quartile of each variable. At the testing phase we evaluated each model with additional missingness similar to the ACTFAST

For the classifier with reliability signal, we utilized the complete dataset but added Gaussian noise with zero mean and variable standard deviation (SD) where the SD values were sampled from a uniform distribution between 1 and 10 standard deviations of each variable. The higher end of SD values is very large, simulating a spectrum of noisy to essentiall missing data. Breast cancer dataset includes values that were measured from images of a fine needle aspitate of a breast mass to describe cell nuclei characteristics. Extracted data includes mean values as well as standard errors and worst value measurements. Due to this nature of variables and them including error rate values, we selected 10 variables representing the mean value measurements only for this experiment. We tested using a 20% test split on the same noisy data.

## 3.5 IMPUTATION TASK

We ran three experiments for imputation by an auto-encoder using the ACTFAST, Breast Cancer, and Boston datasets. For the ACTFAST experiment, we utilized the predictor features described above. We added the MFCL and MFCL+ layers in the place of FC layers at the inputs of an autoencoder imputation system. All parameters of training were similar to the baseline autoencoder described below.

## 3.5.1 BASELINES

The baseline autoencoder for imputation was trained by adding artificial missingess to the input values at random at a ratio of 25%. The loss function at the output layer calculated the mean squared error between the output values and the original values of the artificially removed values. The naturally missing data was included in the training dataset but not included in the loss function due to the absence of a known value to compare to. Then the weights were optimized using an Adam optimizer with learning rate 0.01 and a learning rate scheduler that reduced the learning rate after five epochs of loss not improving. We ran 30 epochs of training for the ACTFAST models with a batch size of 64, and 200 epochs for the Breast Cancer and Boston models. The models were tested using variable artificial missingness.

## 3.6 REGRESSION TASK

We ran one experiment for regression using the Boston dataset where, similar to previous experiments, we added the MFCL and MFCL+ layers in the place of FC layers at the inputs of the networks and used missing flags as modulating signal.

## 3.6.1 BASELINES

For the regressor with missing flags as modulating signal, we introduced artificial non-random missingness into the dataset for training the regressor by removing the highest quartile of each variable. At the testing phase we evaluated each model with additional missingness similar to the classification tasks. The baseline regression networks were two MLPs with matching hidden layer structures. The first one had input variables imputed in a preprocessing step using chained regression (Iterative Imputer). The second one applied constant value imputation (mean value) in addition to concatenating the missing value (input reliability) at the input layer (FC+Mod).

## 3.7 PERFORMANCE EVALUATION

We performed an 80:20 training test split for each dataset to measure the performance for each of the architectures. We performed all our additional missingness tests only on the test split of the datasets. For classification tasks, we utilized area under receiver operating curve (AUROC) and area under precision and recall curve (AUPRC). In the training phase, binary cross-entropy loss was utilized as a cost function. For regression and imputation tasks, we utilized mean squared error loss value as

To compute the margins of error, we conducted 1000 folds of paired bootstrapping for each experiment and computed the 95% confidence intervals for each test case. We show the complete results with confidence intervals in the supplementary information. To test for statistical significance, we calculated repeated measure ANOVA on the bootstrapping results followed by paired t-test between different model pairs with correction for false discovery rate using Benjamini/Yekutieli method.

## 4 RESULTS

## 4.1 CLASSIFICATION WITH MISSING VALUES

Figure 2 plots the test performance of baseline and modified classifiers as a function of additional random missingness. FC+Mod architecture provided the best performance along with VAEAC (on few conditions only) with MFCL and MFCL+ coming close to both of them with a very comparable performance (small effect sizes).

Figure 3 plots classifier performance under increasing amounts of non-random missingness by removing values higher than 80%, 60%, 40%, and 20% quantile from each variable. In this testing case, the performance gain by MFCL is more pronounced where MFCL is outperforms other architectures significantly on the AUPRC of mortality and comes at a close second for Acute Kidney

Injury to VAEAC. DNN+Mod outperforms all networks on the Breast Cancer dataset. This could be due to the relatively small size of the dataset leading to greater of overfitting on larger networks.

Figure 4 shows another example of non-random missingness by removing complete features. This can happen in reality when attempting to test a model using a dataset coming from a different source where some of the measurements are not conducted due to cost or other reasons. We tested this on all the methods except for the missForest which failed when a feature value were completely absent. Here MCFL shows the best performance for AUPRC for Mortality and AKI.

We can see that the modulating architectures are the most consistent especially on the precision and recall measures which is a powerful representative of performance in highly imbalanced data. While some models outperform in certain conditions, they are not as reliable as they fail in other conditions. To our surprise the DNN+Mod model also performed quite well which is a simpler variant of a modulation architecture however it appeared to favor AUROC performance which is not ideal for highly imbalanced datasets.

These results show that MFCL and MFCL+ networks give additional robustness against large quantitites of non-random missingness while still performing strongly well where missingness is low, especially in precision which is most important in highly imbalanced datasets such as ACTFAST.

for ACTFAST, the addition of modulation layer did not add much to the imputation performance in comparison to the normal autoencoder for random removal. However, for the non-random removal, the MFCL/MFCL+ layers showed significantly lower loss indicating higher performance. We also note that the range of losses in this dataset is very different between random and non-random removal which indicates the usefulness of meaningful missingness patterns in predicting the latent representation of the data. It appears that all the networks were able to learn that represnetation but with our architectures learning is better. For other datasets, we found no significant differences which could possibly due to the small size of datasets increasing the overfitting possibility in comparison with the ACTFAST dataset which is orders of magnitude larger.

## 4.3 REGRESSION WITH MISSING VALUES

We utilized the Boston housing dataset as an example of a regression problem with missing data (Figure 6 Supplementary information). In this task, while it appears that MFCL outperformed all other networks significantly in all conditions with VAEAC coming as a close second. Interestingly, the performance either remained the same or improved with more data removed. This could possibly be due to uncorrelated inputs that have very little shared information especially given the high absolute values of loss.

## 4.4 CLASSIFICATION WITH INPUT VALUES WITH VARIABLE RELIABILITY

We tested the modulation layer where input reliability is used as a modulating signal instead of missing flags (Figure 7 Supplementary information). In this condition, the MFCL+ outperformed all other architectures over both AUROC and AUPRC measures significantly. This task performance could be better assessed with real data as it becomes available.

## 5 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION

We propose a new layer for artificial neural networks inspired by biological neuromodulation mechanisms (Harris-Warrick and Marder, 1991). It allows the neural network to alter its weights and thus behavior based on the modulating signal (figure 8 supplementary materials). Our experiments showed that, when added to standard architectures, modulating input layers make predictions more robust to missing and low quality data. In classification, regression, and imputation tasks modulation was most useful when non-random missingness was introduced. However, there was not a consistent benefit to the MFCL versus MFCL+ layer. This could be due to the large number of parameters especially in the last layer of the modulating network which scales with the product

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

of the number of inputs and outputs of the fully connected layer.

Our testing procedure was limited by multiple factors discussed below. First, due to the novelty and flexibility of this model, there are many possible combinations for hyperparameters to explore. In order to limit the hyperparameter search space, we fixed the main network architecture and only varied the modulation network hyperparameters, but in practice there may be interactions between the hyperparameters of the two component networks. One other limitation is the lack of availability of large open tabular datasets with high missingness which limits the ability to generalize our findings. To make our experiments with informative missingness comparable across features, we restricted our input space to numeric variables and discarded categorical variables. Although our method could be applied to missing categorical variables, usually creating a “missing” level is fairly effective. Small technical modifications would also be required to modulate all features derived from encoding a categorical variable in the same way. We expect that future experiments with new real-world datasets will better characterize the performance of this method.

We tested the application of the modulation process only in fully connected layers which are limited by nature in the types of data that they can handle. We intend to test the inclusion of modulation into other architectures such as convolutional layers and gated-recurrent units. It is important to address the issue of the high number of parameters in the modulation network. We did not search over regularization strategies of the modulation network, which may further improve its performance. This is a main benefit of the modulation strategy compared to the conventional strategy of concatenating a missing data indicator to the inputs, which doubles the input space and complicates the search for appropriate architectures and regularization strategies. By separating the two architectures, we can learn a plug-and-play modification for any classification task on the same inputs. It might also prove beneficial to integrate the modulation layer into the existing deep learning imputation models which is an aspect of future investigation especially with its relative stability across different tasks in comparison to the other methods.

One extension of our approach is to add the MFCL in locations in the network beyond the input layer. Preliminary experiments placing MFCL layers deep in the autoencoder experiments did not yield visible improvement (data not shown). The modulating signal could also be any input signal such as context signal in a context switching task which could yield this network useful in multi-task reinforcement learning problems among many other applications (Jovanovich and Phillips, 2018). It can also be useful in compressing multi-task networks by compressing the multiple outputs into one with modulating input acting as a switch to change behavior of the network based on the task in question (Kendall et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020).

In conclusion, we have demonstrated that a modulation architecture could benefit in training neural networks in avenues where data quality is an issue. It can lead to advance the field of MLOps which is concerned with the integration of machine learning systems into production environments and thus fulfilling a big portion of the potential of artificial intelligence systems in advancing state-of-the-art technologies.

## 6 ETHICS STATEMENT

It is important, to understand the possible dangers that lie behind the unethical use of such an architecture where it cause amplification of certain societal biases that are visible in the data. In lower resource settings, marginalized groups have been observed to have more missing data (Chen et al., 2020). Prediction methods not accounting for missing data can produce inaccurate results for these groups and hence, disadvantaging them. Therefore, methods that explicitly account for missing data instead of discarding the data are better in terms of social equity. On the other hand, non-transparency of neural networks, especially that use only small amount of data points for feature values can lead to feature-wise bias amplification (Leino et al., 2018). A solution to mitigate these issues would be to perform a contextual post-processing check on the prediction results. Overall, we believe the proposed algorithm’s positive societal impacts outweigh the negative ones.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a 'Modulated Fully Connected Layer' (MFCL) that uses a secondary MLP to dynamically weight inputs based on reliability signals, aiming to improve neural network robustness to missing or noisy data. While the empirical results on tabular datasets show promise, the methodology lacks theoretical grounding and rigorous architectural validation.

The paper addresses a practical and significant problem in machine learning deployment. The proposed modulation mechanism is intuitive and shows empirical gains in robustness for the tested tabular datasets. However, the work requires more rigorous architectural evaluation and a clearer justification of the modulation function's learning dynamics.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Theoretical Justification for Modulation Dynamics: The paper introduces the modulation function g(m) in Equation 2 without defining its properties, such as constraints on the output range or Lipschitz continuity. Since g(m) directly modifies the weight matrix W, an unconstrained MLP could lead to exploding gradients or unstable weight updates during training, especially if the reliability signal m is noisy. The authors should provide a theoretical argument or empirical evidence demonstrating that the modulation function does not destabilize the primary task learning. Specifically, adding a normalization layer or a sigmoid/tanh activation to the output of g(m) would likely be necessary to ensure the weights remain within a reasonable range.

- Computational Overhead and Scalability Concerns: Section 3.1 describes the MFCL as having weights generated by an MLP. This implies that for every forward pass, the network must compute the output of the modulation MLP before performing the main layer's matrix multiplication. The paper fails to quantify the latency impact of this additional computation, particularly for deep architectures where this layer might be applied repeatedly. Given that the authors mention potential extensions to convolutional and recurrent layers, the scalability of this approach is a major concern that remains unaddressed.

- Sensitivity to Hyperparameter Initialization: The authors acknowledge using grid search for modulation network hyperparameters but do not report the sensitivity of the model to the initialization of the MFCL weights. In architectures where weights are dynamic, the interaction between the initialization of W_0 and the output range of g(m) is critical for convergence. Without a discussion on how these components are initialized—or evidence that the model is robust to different initialization seeds—the reproducibility of the reported performance gains is questionable.

- Incomplete Evaluation of Non-Random Missingness (MNAR): The paper claims robustness against non-random missingness (MNAR) in Section 4.1, yet the experimental setup for MNAR involves removing values based on quantiles. This is a specific form of missingness that is highly correlated with the feature values themselves. It is unclear if the modulation layer is actually learning to compensate for the missing information or simply learning a bias correction for the truncated distribution. To support the claim of robustness to MNAR, the authors should test on datasets where missingness is driven by unobserved latent variables, which is more representative of real-world healthcare scenarios.

- Overfitting Risk in Small Datasets: The authors observe that the MFCL performs differently across datasets, with smaller datasets (like Breast Cancer) showing potential overfitting. The modulation layer effectively increases the parameter count of the network significantly, as the modulation MLP must learn to map the reliability signal to the entire weight space of the FC layer. The paper lacks a formal analysis of the parameter-to-data ratio or a comparison of model complexity relative to standard baselines. A more thorough ablation study on the size of the modulation MLP would clarify whether the observed robustness is a result of the modulation mechanism or simply an increase in model capacity.

- Absence of sensitivity analysis regarding modulation network capacity: The paper introduces a modulation layer that significantly increases the parameter count, yet it does not systematically analyze how the complexity of the modulation MLP affects the primary task. It is unclear if the robustness gains are due to the architectural innovation or merely the increased capacity of the model. To address this, the authors should perform an ablation study on the modulation network's depth and width, specifically comparing the performance of a standard MLP with increased hidden units against the MFCL architecture. This would verify that the modulation mechanism itself, rather than just additional parameters, provides the claimed robustness.

- Lack of empirical validation for the 'biological neuromodulation' claim: The authors motivate their architecture by referencing biological neuromodulation, yet they do not demonstrate that the learned modulation signals actually exhibit properties consistent with this biological analogy. A simple analysis showing the correlation between the reliability signal and the learned weight adjustments would clarify whether the layer is performing the intended task-specific weighting. Providing a visualization of the modulation weight distribution for different levels of input reliability would bridge the gap between the biological inspiration and the actual empirical behavior of the model.

- Missing computational latency benchmark for real-time deployment: The paper explicitly claims that this architecture is suitable for deployment in real-time healthcare settings, yet it provides no metrics on the inference latency introduced by the modulation MLP. Because the modulation signal must be processed before the main layer's weight matrix can be generated, the overhead could be non-negligible for high-frequency or large-scale inputs. The authors should include a table comparing the inference time (in milliseconds) of a standard MLP versus the MFCL and MFCL+ architectures on the ACTFAST dataset to substantiate the claim of practical utility in real-time environments.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a formal analysis or empirical evidence regarding the stability of the modulation function g(m), including constraints on its output range.

- Include a detailed latency and parameter count analysis comparing the MFCL architecture to standard baselines to address real-time deployment concerns.

- Expand the MNAR evaluation to include missingness patterns that are not purely quantile-based, such as missingness dependent on latent variables or external context.

- Perform a sensitivity analysis on the initialization of the modulation layer to demonstrate that the performance gains are not dependent on specific random seeds.

comments.title: Inaccurate Claim Regarding Masking in Tabular Data

comments.explanation: This claim is inaccurate. Masking is a standard objective in tabular deep learning, utilized in architectures like TabTransformer and various denoising autoencoders. You should rephrase this to acknowledge existing literature on tabular masking while clarifying how your specific modulation approach differs from these established pre-training techniques.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Attention Mechanism Scalability
comments.quote: attention networks employ a complex feedback mechanism to assign the attention distribution using the input sequence and the query and is thus difficult to scale for long time-varying inputs
comments.explanation: The assertion that attention mechanisms rely on a 'feedback mechanism' is incorrect. Modern attention layers, such as those in Transformers, use parallelizable feed-forward operations. While quadratic complexity is a known challenge, it is addressed by linear-time variants, not by the absence of feedback. Please correct this description to accurately reflect the operation of attention layers.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Bias Term Modulation
comments.quote: We propose a modulated fully connected layer (MFCL) where weights are made variable by replacing  $\mathbf{
comments.explanation: The paper defines the modulation of the weight matrix W, but it is silent on the bias term b. If the bias remains static while the weights are modulated by the reliability signal m, the output may become dominated by the bias for certain values of m. comments.title: Statistical Test Mismatch for Bootstrapping
comments.quote: To test for statistical significance, we calculated repeated measure ANOVA on the bootstrapping results followed by paired t-test between different model pairs
comments.explanation: Applying repeated measures ANOVA to bootstrapped samples is statistically inappropriate because resampled statistics are not independent observations. This approach risks inflating Type I error rates. A more robust method would be to construct confidence intervals for the performance differences directly from the bootstrap distribution and check if they exclude zero.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Missingness as a Reliability Subset
comments.quote: We can think of missing values as a special case included in reliability where missing implies completely unreliable measurement. For the sake of clarity, we test the cases of missing values and noisy values separately rather than combining them.
comments.explanation: Defining missingness as a subset of reliability but testing them separately creates a logical contradiction. If missingness is a special case of the reliability signal, the model should handle it within a unified framework. Please clarify whether the modulation network parameters are shared across these tasks or if distinct hyperparameter tuning is required for each, as this impacts the claim of a general-purpose robustness layer.

comments.title: Inconsistent Naming of Baseline Architectures
comments.quote: The last model applied constant value imputation (mean value) in addition to concatenating an indicator variable for missing values at the input layer (FC+Mod).
comments.explanation: The label 'FC+Mod' is used here for a mean-imputation baseline, which creates significant confusion given that the proposed architecture is also referred to as a modulation layer. Please rename this baseline (e.g., 'Mean+Indicator') to distinguish it clearly from the MFCL architecture.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a modulated fully connected layer to improve robustness against missing and noisy data. The biological inspiration is compelling, and the joint training setup is conceptually straightforward. However, the experimental validation relies on simplistic missingness simulations, and the comparison baselines do not adequately represent the current state of robust deep learning. The modulation network's parameterization raises significant overfitting concerns that the authors acknowledge but do not resolve.

The proposal addresses a practical problem in deploying models to production environments where data quality is unpredictable. The authors provide a clear mathematical formulation and test the method across classification, regression, and imputation tasks. The experimental design covers multiple datasets and missingness patterns, and the statistical evaluation uses bootstrapping and ANOVA. Despite these strengths, the method's scalability and real-world applicability are constrained by its heavy parameterization and limited architectural scope.

Overview issues:

- Overstated Novelty and Weak Baselines: The paper compares the MFCL primarily to imputation-based methods like GAIN, VAEAC, and missForest, alongside a simple mean-imputation baseline with missing indicators. This comparison framework is misaligned with the stated goal of creating a robust architectural modification. If the aim is to handle missing data without preprocessing, the appropriate baselines are other robust training techniques such as MC Dropout, Bayesian neural networks, or modern robust loss functions. Comparing a novel layer directly to imputation algorithms conflates two different problem-solving paradigms. The authors should include contemporary robust deep learning methods to properly position the MFCL and demonstrate its unique advantages.

- Parameter Explosion in the Modulation Network: Equation 2 defines the modulated weight matrix as W_mod = g(m), where g is an MLP mapping a reliability vector directly to a full weight matrix. This design creates a parameter count that scales with the product of input and output dimensions, which the discussion section acknowledges but treats as a minor inconvenience. On small datasets like Breast Cancer, this parameter-heavy modulation network will likely overfit, severely limiting generalization. The paper lacks any discussion of parameter-efficient alternatives, such as diagonal scaling, low-rank factorization, or gating mechanisms. Implementing a constrained modulation function would drastically reduce overfitting risk and improve scalability to larger models.

- Simplistic Missingness Simulations vs. Real-World Mechanisms: The introduction correctly identifies that healthcare data often suffers from MAR or MNAR patterns, yet the experimental design only tests MCAR (random dropping) and a crude non-random scheme based on fixed quantile cutoffs. Real-world informative missingness depends on observed or unobserved patient characteristics, which a static quantile removal cannot capture. Consequently, the reported robustness gains may not hold under realistic clinical data distributions. The authors need to simulate or test on datasets with structured missingness patterns that reflect actual clinical workflows. Without this, the claims about deployment in real-time healthcare settings remain speculative.

- Lack of Training Stability Analysis: The methodology states that the modulation network is jointly learned with the main task using backpropagation. Dynamically computing weights per sample based on m introduces a complex optimization landscape that the paper does not analyze. Updating weights for every sample can cause severe optimization instability or vanishing gradients, especially when reliability scores are noisy or poorly calibrated. The authors should include a training dynamics analysis, such as gradient norm tracking or learning rate sensitivity tests, to demonstrate that the joint training procedure is stable across different missingness rates. This analysis is essential to prove the method is viable for practical training pipelines.

- Marginal Gains and Statistical Significance: Section 4.1 reports that MFCL and MFCL+ perform comparably to baselines, with only small effect sizes in most classification tasks. The strongest gains appear in AUPRC under non-random missingness, but the absolute performance differences are often within the reported confidence intervals. The paper makes broad claims about enabling AI deployment in production environments based on these marginal improvements. The authors should temper their conclusions to match the empirical evidence or include ablation studies that isolate the specific conditions where the modulation layer provides decisive advantages. Reporting effect sizes alongside p-values would give readers a clearer picture of practical utility.

- Limited Architectural Scope: The modulation layer is exclusively tested on fully connected layers at the input level. The authors acknowledge this limitation and list convolutional or recurrent extensions as future work. However, the core contribution relies on the assumption that input reliability modulation generalizes across architectures. Since the modulation network's parameterization is tightly coupled to the input dimension, extending it to CNNs or RNNs requires non-trivial architectural redesigns. The paper should at least provide a conceptual extension or a small-scale experiment on a different architecture to validate the generalizability of the modulation principle.

- Missing reliability estimation mechanism for deployment: The paper claims the method enables deployment in real-time healthcare settings, yet provides no mechanism for computing the modulating signal m from raw, uncurated data. In experiments, m is hand-crafted using missing flags or artificially injected noise standard deviations. Readers will wonder how the system operates when reliability scores are unknown at inference time. This matters because a deployment-ready architecture must either estimate reliability end-to-end or include a calibration module. The authors should add a section detailing how to estimate m from historical data or sensor metadata, perhaps by training a lightweight auxiliary network to predict reliability, and report the latency overhead of this estimation step.

- No worked special case to calibrate intuition: The modulation function g(m) is defined as a generic MLP mapping a reliability vector to a weight matrix. Without a concrete parametric example, it is difficult to understand how the layer behaves under extreme reliability conditions or how the non-linearity in g shapes the weight updates. A natural question is whether the authors can compute the layer's output for a simple 2D linear model with binary missing indicators. Providing a fully specified toy example would let readers trace how the modulation network down-weights noisy inputs and verify that the restricted structure actually limits the effective hypothesis space as claimed.

- Missing computational trade-off analysis for efficiency claims: The introduction and discussion repeatedly emphasize that the modulation layer saves processing time by skipping imputation preprocessing. However, the paper never reports the computational cost of the modulation network itself. The weight generation MLP scales with the product of input and output dimensions, which the authors acknowledge as a parameter explosion risk. Readers will wonder if the inference latency savings from skipping imputation are actually offset by the cost of computing g(m) for every sample. The authors should include a table comparing FLOPs, memory footprint, and inference latency between the MFCL, standard FC layers, and the imputation baselines to substantiate the efficiency claim.

- Underdeveloped connection to existing dynamic weight methods: The paper states the approach is superficially similar to attention mechanisms but dismisses attention as complex. It does not formally position MFCL within the family of dynamic weight generation methods like hypernetworks or conditional batch normalization. If the claim is that MFCL offers a simpler, more stable alternative, the authors should verify that MFCL recovers standard multiplicative gating as a special case when g(m) is constrained to be linear or diagonal. Without this comparison, the theoretical positioning remains vague and the architectural novelty is hard to assess.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a conceptually interesting modulation layer but requires substantial experimental and theoretical improvements to support its claims. The parameter explosion in the modulation network, simplistic missingness simulations, and weak baseline comparisons must be addressed before the method can be considered for publication.

Revision targets:

- Replace or supplement imputation baselines with contemporary robust training methods (e.g., MC Dropout, Bayesian NNs, robust loss functions) to properly evaluate the architectural contribution.

- Introduce parameter-efficient modulation strategies, such as diagonal scaling or low-rank factorization, and demonstrate their impact on overfitting and training stability.

- Simulate realistic MAR/MNAR missingness patterns that reflect clinical decision-making, rather than relying solely on random dropping and fixed quantile removal.

- Provide a detailed analysis of training dynamics, including gradient norm tracking and learning rate sensitivity, to verify the stability of joint backpropagation through the modulation network.

- Temper the deployment claims to align with the reported marginal effect sizes, or include ablation studies that isolate the specific conditions where the modulation layer provides decisive advantages.

comments.title: Misattribution of classical imputation methods to Buck (1960)
comments.quote: data. Classical simple methods of imputation include constant values (e.g. mean imputation), hot deck, k-nearest neighbor, and others *(Buck, 1
comments.explanation: The citation to Buck (1960) for mean imputation and hot deck is historically inaccurate. Buck's paper introduced a regression-based approach for missing multivariate data suitable for early computers, not mean imputation or hot deck imputation. Mean imputation predates Buck by decades, and hot deck methods originate from survey sampling literature. Citing Buck for these specific techniques misrepresents the historical development of imputation algorithms. Replace the citation with appropriate statistical textbooks or survey sampling references that actually describe mean and hot deck imputation.

comments.title: Outdated dismissal of attention mechanisms for scalability
comments.quote:
ach is superficially similar to attention mechanisms: the lower quality inputs receive less importance, but attention networks employ a complex feedback mechanism to assign the attention distribution using the input sequence and the query and is thus difficult to scale for long time-varying inputs *(Kim et al., 2017)*.

<!-- PAG
comments.explanation: The claim that attention mechanisms are inherently difficult to scale for long time-varying inputs relies on an outdated characterization of the field. While early additive attention models faced quadratic complexity, a substantial body of recent work has introduced linear, sparse, and kernelized attention variants that scale efficiently to long sequences. Citing Kim et al. (2017) to support this scalability limitation is insufficient given the rapid advancements in efficient attention architectures over the past few years. Dismissing attention-based methods without acknowledging these modern, scalable alternatives undermines the paper's positioning relative to contemporary architectures. Update this comparison to reflect current efficient attention methods or clarify why the specific modulation layer offers distinct advantages over modern linear attention variants.

comments.title: Misapplied skip connection terminology and flawed stability justification
comments.quote: tron. Another variant of the MFCL (MFCL+) adds a skip connection to the transfer step and modifies the weights  $(\mathbf{W_0})$  of a starting network,
comments.explanation: The authors incorrectly label the operation in Equation (3) as a 'skip connection.' In deep learning literature, a skip (or residual) connection adds the input activation to the layer's output activation, not a fixed matrix to the weight matrix. Equation (3) instead describes a residual weight initialization where dynamically generated weights g(m) are offset by a pre-trained matrix W0. Furthermore, the stated justification that MFCL+ avoids instabilities 'by having the entire layer weights variable' is logically inconsistent, as the original MFCL in Equation (2) already possesses entirely variable weights. The actual source of stability in MFCL+ is the pre-trained initialization W0, which anchors the modulation network and prevents unbounded weight updates when reliability scores indicate high data quality. Rewrite 'adds a skip connection to the transfer step' as 'uses a residual weight initialization' and revise the stability justification to explain that W0 anchors the optimization landscape.

comments.title: Ambiguous mapping from modulating signal to weight matrix dimensions
comments.quote: here  $m$  is the modulating signal input and  $g$  is the function that is defined by a multilayer perceptron. An
comments.explanation: A standard multilayer perceptron g takes a vector input and produces a vector output. However, W_mod and W_mod+ are weight matrices that must match the dimensions of the original layer's weights. The text does not specify how g maps the scalar or vector modulating signal m to a matrix of the required shape. Clarify whether g includes a reshape operation, or if m is a vector of the same dimension as the flattened weight matrix, and how g's output is structured to match W. Add a description of g's output dimensionality and any reshaping steps after 'defined by a multilayer perceptron'.

comments.title: Unspecified baseline handling of missing data
comments.quote: For baseline comparison, we employed models with matching architectures while swapping the first fully-connected layer with a MFCL/MFCL+.
comments.explanation: Standard fully-connected layers require complete input vectors and will crash or produce undefined gradients when encountering missing values. The description of the baselines omits how they process incomplete data, which is essential for a fair comparison. If the baselines rely on mean imputation, k-NN, or missing indicators, this preprocessing step must be explicitly stated. Add a sentence detailing the exact missing-data preprocessing applied to each baseline model.

comments.title: Ambiguous experiment distribution and contradictory dataset usage
comments.quote: We ran five experiments for classification using the ACTFAST and Breast Cancer datasets. Four experiments utilized the missing flags as a modulating signal; in the last experiment, we utilized input reliability as a modulating signal.
comments.explanation: The text states five experiments were run using two real datasets, but it is unclear how they are distributed. More importantly, the fifth experiment relies on artificially added noise to quantify reliability, which contradicts the premise that the experiments use the real ACTFAST and Breast Cancer datasets. If the fifth experiment uses synthetic noise, it cannot simultaneously be classified as using the real clinical data. Specify the exact dataset allocation for each of the five experiments and clarify whether the noise-based experiment applies to a separate synthetic dataset or a modified version of one of the real datasets.

comments.title: Inverted reliability metric definition
comments.quote: Reliability was quantified by the standard deviation of the noise that was artificially added to the signal.
comments.explanation: Stating that reliability is quantified by the standard deviation is mathematically inverted. A higher standard deviation indicates greater noise and thus lower reliability. The modulation signal needs to map high noise to low reliability values, which requires using the inverse of the variance or a signal-to-noise ratio. This phrasing will confuse readers trying to reproduce the modulation network's input. Rewrite 'Reliability was quantified by the standard deviation of the noise that was artificially added to the signal.' as 'Reliability was derived from the inverse of the noise variance (or signal-to-noise ratio) of the artificially added noise.'

comments.title: Enumeration mismatch between stated count and listed models
comments.quote:
for the ACTFAST and Breast Cancer datasets were four MLPs with matching hidden layer structures that were fed imputed values using different algorithms, namely: chained regression with Bayesian ridge regularization (Scikit Learn Iterative Imputer), missForest algorithm *(Stekhoven and Bühlmann, 2012)*, GAIN *(Yoon et al., 2018)*, and VAEAC *(Ivanov et al., 2019)*. We also tested one graphical network model GRAPE *(You et al., 2020)*. The last model applied constant value imputation (mean value) in addition to concatenating an indicator variable for missing values at the input layer (FC+Mod).

##### ACTFAST

We
comments.explanation: The opening sentence explicitly claims there are 'four MLPs,' but the subsequent text enumerates six distinct baselines: four imputation-based MLPs, one graphical network (GRAPE), and one mean-imputation model with missingness indicators (FC+Mod). This discrepancy obscures the exact composition of the baseline suite. Rewrite the first sentence to accurately reflect the total number of models, such as 'six baseline models,' and clarify which are MLPs versus other architectures to prevent reader confusion.

comments.title: Conflation of high-noise corruption with missing data
comments.quote: The higher end of SD values is very large, simulating a spectrum of noisy to essentially missing data.
comments.explanation: Adding Gaussian noise with a standard deviation up to 10 times the original variable's SD results in a signal-to-noise ratio of 1:100 at the upper bound. This creates heavily corrupted data, but it does not simulate 'missing data.' Missing data implies the absence of a value, whereas this procedure replaces the value with a highly noisy observation. Replace 'essentially missing data' with 'heavily corrupted data' or explicitly explain how this noise injection maps to the missingness framework used elsewhere in the paper.

comments.title: Non-standard terminology and statistical pipeline ambiguity
comments.quote:
on metric.

To compute the margins of error, we conducted 1000 folds of paired bootstrapping for each experiment and computed the  $95\%$  confidence intervals for each test case. We show the complete results with confidence intervals in the supplementary information. To test for statistical significance, we calculated repeated measure ANOVA on the bootstrapping results followed by paired t-test between different model pairs with correction for false discovery rate using Benjamini/Yek
comments.explanation: The phrase '1000 folds of paired bootstrapping' conflates cross-validation terminology with resampling terminology. Bootstrap does not partition data into folds; it draws random samples with replacement, so 'iterations' or 'resamples' is the correct term. More importantly, running parametric tests (ANOVA and t-tests) directly on the bootstrap distributions is statistically problematic. Bootstrap samples are drawn from the empirical distribution of the test set, not the underlying population. Their variance is fixed by the observed data, so treating them as independent draws for hypothesis testing yields anti-conservative p-values that do not reflect genuine statistical significance. Standard practice is to use bootstrapping strictly for confidence interval estimation, while performing significance tests on the original test-set metric values or via permutation tests. Rewrite the methodology to specify that ANOVA and t-tests are applied to the actual test-set scores, and reserve bootstrapping for interval calculation.

comments.title: Inconsistent quantile ordering for increasing missingness
comments.quote: re 3 plots classifier performance under increasing amounts of non-random missingness by removing values higher than  $80\%$ ,  $60\%$ ,  $40\%$ , and  $20\%$  quantile from each variable.
comments.explanation: The text claims to plot performance under increasing amounts of missingness, but the listed quantiles (80%, 60%, 40%, 20%) correspond to decreasing proportions of removed values (20%, 40%, 60%, 80%). This ordering directly contradicts the stated progression of missingness rates. Readers will be confused about whether the x-axis represents decreasing quantile thresholds or increasing missingness rates. Rewrite 'Figure 3 plots classifier performance under increasing amounts of non-random missingness by removing values higher than 80%, 60%, 40%, and 20% quantile from each variable.' as 'Figure 3 plots classifier performance under increasing amounts of non-random missingness by removing values higher than the 20%, 40%, 60%, and 80% quantiles from each variable.'

comments.title: Missing training protocol for imputation networks
comments.quote: tested the imputation networks by introducing  $10\%$  missingness in the test datasets and measuring the mean squared error. We found that
comments.explanation: The sentence describes the evaluation step but omits how the autoencoder was trained. To evaluate imputation on a test set with missing values, the model must have been trained on data with a specific missingness pattern. Without specifying the training masking strategy or whether the training set also contained 10% missing values, the experimental setup is ambiguous. Add a brief clause clarifying the training data preparation to complete the method description.

comments.title: Causal leap regarding loss ranges
comments.quote: We also note that the range of losses in this dataset is very different between random and non-random removal which indicates the usefulness of meaningful missingness patterns in predicting the latent representation of the data.
comments.explanation: The authors attribute the difference in loss ranges directly to the 'usefulness of meaningful missingness patterns.' However, loss magnitude is heavily influenced by the scale of the target variables and the difficulty of reconstruction under different masking schemes. A larger loss under non-random removal could simply mean the missing values are concentrated in high-variance features, not that the patterns are inherently more useful for prediction. Rewrite to decouple loss magnitude from pattern utility, or clarify how the authors isolated the effect of missingness structure from feature scale.

comments.title: Non-sequitur explanation for performance improvement
comments.quote: Interestingly, the performance either remained the same or improved with more data removed. This could possibly be due to uncorrelated inputs that have very little shared information especially given the high absolute values of loss.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that performance improves or stays stable as more data is removed, but the provided justification is logically disconnected. Dropping uncorrelated inputs can reduce model variance or act as implicit feature selection, which might explain stability or slight gains. However, citing 'high absolute values of loss' as a contributing factor is a non-sequitur. Loss magnitude depends on the target scale and error distribution, not on the benefit of removing noisy features. The authors should remove the reference to loss values and instead clarify whether the improvement stems from regularization effects, noise filtering, or dataset-specific properties. If the improvement is marginal, they should report the exact metric changes to avoid overstating the finding.

comments.title: Absolute performance claim lacks qualification
comments.quote: In this condition, the MFCL+ outperformed all other architectures over both AUROC and AUPRC measures significantly.
comments.explanation: This sentence makes an absolute claim that is rarely true in empirical deep learning studies and contradicts the broader findings that performance gains are often marginal or dataset-specific. Readers will question which specific baselines and datasets support this statement. Absolute generalizations obscure the actual experimental scope and make the results difficult to reproduce or compare. Rewrite 'In this condition, the MFCL+ outperformed all other architectures over both AUROC and AUPRC measures significantly.' as 'In this condition, MFCL+ outperformed [specific baselines] on [specific datasets] by [X]% in AUROC and [Y]% in AUPRC.' because specifying the exact scope replaces an unsupported generalization with verifiable empirical evidence.

comments.title: Weight modulation does not compress outputs
comments.quote: It can also be useful in compressing multi-task networks by compressing the multiple outputs into one with modulating input acting as a switch to change behavior of the network based on the task in question
comments.explanation: The proposed mechanism does not support the stated compression capability. A weight-modulation layer dynamically scales or shifts existing weights based on an input signal; it does not project, pool, or reduce the dimensionality of network outputs. Claiming that the layer 'compresses the multiple outputs into one' conflates weight adaptation with architectural routing or dimensionality reduction. Without a formal derivation or architectural diagram showing how output dimensions are collapsed, this extension claim is mechanistically unsupported. Remove 'by compressing the multiple outputs into one with modulating input acting as a switch to change behavior of the network based on the task in question' because the weight-modulation mechanism does not inherently reduce output dimensionality or perform output compression.

comments.title: Conclusion overstates MLOps relevance
comments.quote: It can lead to advance the field of MLOps which is concerned with the integration of machine learning systems into production environments and thus fulfilling a big portion of the potential of artificial intelligence systems in advancing state-of-the-art technologies.
comments.explanation: This concluding assertion significantly overreaches the empirical evidence presented. The experiments evaluate resilience on small tabular datasets with synthetically injected missingness, lacking any analysis of inference latency, calibration, drift detection, or pipeline integration that define MLOps. Asserting that the method fulfills a 'big portion of the potential' of AI systems in production environments is not supported by the reported results. The conclusion should be grounded in the actual scope of the findings rather than speculative deployment claims. Rewrite 'It can lead to advance the field of MLOps which is concerned with the integration of machine learning systems into production environments and thus fulfilling a big portion of the potential of artificial intelligence systems in advancing state-of-the-art technologies.' as 'These findings suggest the modulation architecture may improve resilience to missing data in standard feedforward networks, warranting further investigation into its deployment in production environments.'

comments.title: Imprecise comparison to missing indicators
comments.quote: This is a main benefit of the modulation strategy compared to the conventional strategy of concatenating a missing data indicator to the inputs, which doubles the input space and complicates the search for appropriate architectures and regularization strategies.
comments.explanation: The assertion that concatenating missing indicators 'doubles the input space' is mathematically imprecise and misrepresents the comparison. Adding a binary indicator per feature linearly increases the feature vector dimension from N to 2N, but it does not double the representational capacity or the architectural search space. Modern deep learning frameworks handle higher-dimensional inputs without fundamentally complicating architecture search. The authors should replace this vague geometric claim with a concrete analysis of parameter counts, training stability, or empirical performance differences between the two approaches. Rewrite 'which doubles the input space and complicates the search for appropriate architectures and regularization strategies' as 'which increases the input dimensionality and requires careful regularization to prevent overfitting'.

comments.title: Overstated claim on social equity and missing data
comments.quote: Therefore, methods that explicitly account for missing data instead of discarding the data are better in terms of social equity.
comments.explanation: This assertion is too broad and lacks necessary qualification. Accounting for missing data does not inherently improve social equity, especially when missingness is MNAR and systematically correlated with protected attributes. If marginalized groups are more likely to have missing values for specific features, a model that accounts for missingness may still encode the correlation between missingness and the protected attribute, potentially amplifying bias rather than mitigating it. The authors should clarify that their method addresses bias under MCAR or MAR assumptions, and acknowledge that true equity requires additional fairness-aware modeling or auditing beyond missing data handling. Rewrite 'Therefore, methods that explicitly account for missing data instead of discarding the data are better in terms of social equity.' as 'Therefore, methods that explicitly account for missing data can reduce bias under MCAR or MAR assumptions, though additional fairness auditing remains necessary for MNAR scenarios.'

comments.title: Discussion claims universal applicability not established by formal definitions
comments.quote: This is a main benefit of the modulation strategy compared to the conventional strategy of concatenating a missing data indicator to the inputs, which doubles the input space and complicates the search for appropriate architectures and regularization strategies. By separating the two architectures, we can learn a plug-and-play modification for any classification task on the same inputs.
comments.explanation: The formal results section only provides the architectural definitions for the MFCL and MFCL+ layers (Equations 1–3). It does not include any theoretical analysis, convergence guarantees, or empirical validation demonstrating that this modular design functions as a 'plug-and-play' solution for 'any classification task.' The claim of universal applicability and the stated benefit over input concatenation are design motivations and empirical observations, not formal results. Rewrite the quoted text as 'The modular design allows for straightforward integration into existing architectures, which we evaluate empirically across several classification tasks' because the formal definitions alone do not establish general applicability or plug-and-play functionality across all tasks.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a modulated fully connected layer (MFCL) for neural networks, inspired by biological neuromodulation, to improve robustness to missing and noisy data. The core idea is to replace fixed weights with a function of a reliability signal, learned jointly with the main task. The paper presents experiments on classification, regression, and imputation tasks using several datasets.

The paper addresses a relevant problem—robustness to data quality issues—and introduces a biologically inspired architectural modification. The experimental setup covers multiple tasks and datasets, and the results show some promising improvements, particularly under non-random missingness. However, the paper has significant weaknesses in its evaluation methodology, lack of theoretical grounding, and insufficient comparison with state-of-the-art methods, which limit its contribution.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient comparison with state-of-the-art methods for missing data: The paper compares the proposed MFCL/MFCL+ layers against several baselines, but the selection is incomplete. The literature context highlights GAIN, MIDA, and MIWAE as key deep learning methods for missing data, yet only GAIN is included. VAEAC and GRAPE are included, but the paper does not compare against recent methods like MIDA or MIWAE. Additionally, the paper does not compare against simpler but effective methods like MissForest or MICE in all experiments (e.g., MissForest is missing from some figures). The omission of these strong baselines makes it difficult to assess whether the modulation layer offers a genuine advantage over existing approaches. The paper should include a more comprehensive set of baselines, particularly those that are standard in the missing data literature.

- Lack of theoretical justification for the modulation mechanism: The paper claims that the modulation layer is inspired by biological neuromodulation, but it does not provide any theoretical reasoning for why this specific mechanism should improve robustness to missing data. There is no analysis of how the modulation function g(m) should be structured, what properties it should have, or under what conditions it would be beneficial. The paper also does not discuss whether the modulation layer could be subsumed by simply concatenating reliability scores to the input (which is already a baseline, FC+Mod). The theoretical connection to Bayesian inference or attention mechanisms is mentioned but not developed. The paper would benefit from a formal analysis, perhaps showing that the modulation layer can learn an optimal weighting scheme under certain missingness patterns, or that it is equivalent to a specific form of attention.

- Potential overfitting to synthetic missingness patterns and limited evaluation on diverse data quality issues: The paper primarily evaluates on missing data, with only one experiment on noisy data (Section 4.4). The missingness patterns are artificially introduced (random, non-random by quantile, and complete feature removal). While these are reasonable, the paper does not test on real-world missingness patterns, which may have complex dependencies. Moreover, the paper does not evaluate on other common data quality issues such as outliers, label errors, or distribution shift. The claim that the method increases robustness against 'data quality issues' is too broad given the limited scope of evaluation. The paper should either narrow its claims to missing data specifically or include experiments on a wider range of data quality problems.

- Absence of ablation studies to isolate the contribution of the modulation layer: The paper does not perform ablation studies to understand which components of the modulation layer are responsible for the observed improvements. For example, it is unclear whether the benefit comes from the modulation mechanism itself, the increased number of parameters, or the specific architecture of the modulation network (MLP). The paper also does not compare against a simple learned weighting scheme (e.g., a single learned weight per input feature) to see if the complexity of the MLP is necessary. The MFCL+ variant adds a skip connection, but the paper does not systematically compare MFCL vs MFCL+ across all conditions. Ablation studies are essential to demonstrate that the proposed design choices are necessary and sufficient for the claimed robustness.

- Assumption that missingness is random or ignorable without testing for non-random patterns: The paper acknowledges that missingness can be non-random (MNAR) and tests non-random missingness by removing high quantile values or entire features. However, these artificial patterns may not reflect real MNAR mechanisms, where missingness depends on unobserved variables. The paper does not discuss whether the modulation layer would still be effective under more realistic MNAR scenarios, such as when missingness is correlated with the outcome. The paper should either provide a theoretical argument for why the method should work under MNAR, or explicitly state the limitations of the evaluation in this regard.

- Statistical significance testing is insufficiently reported: The paper states that repeated measures ANOVA and paired t-tests with FDR correction were used, but the results of these tests are not reported in the main text or figures. The figures show 95% confidence intervals from bootstrapping, but it is not clear which comparisons are statistically significant. For example, in Figure 2, the confidence intervals for different methods overlap substantially, making it difficult to conclude that MFCL outperforms baselines. The paper should report p-values or effect sizes for key comparisons, or at least indicate which differences are statistically significant in the figures.

- Limited analysis of learned modulation weights or patterns: The paper does not analyze what the modulation network learns. For instance, it would be informative to visualize the learned weights as a function of missingness or noise level, to see if the network is indeed up/down-weighting inputs as intended. Without such analysis, it is unclear whether the modulation layer is actually performing the hypothesized function or simply acting as a complex black-box adaptation. The paper should include an analysis of the learned modulation weights, perhaps by showing how they vary with input reliability or missingness patterns.

- No demonstration that modulation layer is non-vacuous: The paper claims the modulation layer improves robustness to missing data, but never shows a concrete example where a standard neural network fails and the MFCL succeeds. A minimal demonstration would be a synthetic dataset with a known optimal decision boundary, where missing features make the standard network's performance collapse while the MFCL maintains accuracy. For instance, generate data from a simple logistic model with two features, mask one feature completely at test time, and show that the MFCL learns to rely on the remaining feature while a standard MLP with mean imputation cannot. Without such a controlled example, it is unclear whether the reported improvements are due to the modulation mechanism or to confounding factors like increased model capacity.

- Missing worked parametric example: The paper would benefit from computing the modulation function g(m) for a simple, fully-specified model. For example, consider a linear regression with one predictor x and a binary reliability flag m. The paper could derive the optimal g(m) under squared error loss when x is missing with probability p. This would show whether the modulation layer can recover the optimal imputation strategy (e.g., using the conditional mean when x is missing) and would calibrate reader intuition about the method's behavior. Currently, the paper provides no such analytical example, leaving the mechanism entirely opaque.

- No discussion of estimation or implementation challenges: The paper introduces a modulation network g(m) that is an MLP, but does not discuss how to choose its architecture, regularization, or training procedure. The number of parameters in g(m) scales with the product of input and output dimensions of the modulated layer, which can be large. The paper acknowledges this only in passing (Section 5) but provides no guidance on preventing overfitting or on computational costs. For a method that claims practical relevance, the absence of any practical implementation advice — such as recommended MLP depth, weight decay, or learning rate scheduling for the modulation network — is a notable gap. The paper should include a brief discussion of these issues, perhaps with a sensitivity analysis.

- Underdeveloped claim of general robustness to data quality issues: The abstract and introduction claim the method increases robustness against 'data quality issues', but the experiments only test missing values and one noisy-input scenario. Other common issues like label noise, outliers, or distribution shift are not evaluated. The paper should either narrow its claims to missing data specifically (and adjust the title and abstract accordingly) or include experiments on at least one additional data quality problem. For example, adding random label flips to the Breast Cancer dataset and measuring AUROC would be a simple way to test whether the modulation layer helps beyond missing data.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a more comprehensive set of baselines, including MIDA, MIWAE, and a simple learned weighting scheme, to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed modulation layer.

- Include ablation studies that isolate the contribution of the modulation mechanism, e.g., by comparing against a single learned weight per feature and by varying the complexity of the modulation network.

- Provide a theoretical justification for why the modulation layer should improve robustness, perhaps by connecting it to optimal weighting under a missing data model.

- Report statistical significance tests (p-values or effect sizes) for key comparisons, and clearly indicate which differences are significant in the figures.

- Include an analysis of the learned modulation weights to show that the network is indeed adapting to data quality as intended.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in Equation (2)
comments.quote:
We propose a modulated fully connected layer (MFCL) where weights are made variable by replacing  $\mathbf{W}$  by  $\mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{mod}}$  (Figure 1) where

$$
\mathbf {W} _ {\mathbf {m o d}} = g (m), \tag {2}
$$
